## Problem Statement

Whatfix’s current troubleshooting experience is not content creator-friendly and leads to an over-reliance on Whatfix Support/Solutions teams.

Parameters such as complexity of the issue, availability of application access, and inconsistent behavior across users lead to screen time requests from Whatfix. Screen time with either the content creator or the end user may not always be feasible, leading to a broken experience.

Providing real-time, contextual diagnostics to content creators, in a user-friendly manner would improve the overall content creation/testing experience and self-serviceability.

## Proposed Solution

Build/incorporate an inspector panel in Studio that encompasses the length and breadth of Whatfix features and provides contextual information in real-time

### Target Audience

* Content creators  
* QA teams/testers  
* Customer-facing teams in Whatfix

### How would this help?

* Provide a robust, visual debugging experience  
* Aid in gathering context, understanding the impacted area/feature, and recommend potential solutions/fixes just-in-time  
* Help address/gather context on issues that may be isolated to specific users, environments or systems  
* Reduce screen time with content creators and improve TAT for overall issue resolution

## As-is Behaviour

### **Content Creators**

Available troubleshooting avenues for content creators are:

1. Self-serve via Support KB \- [https://support.whatfix.com/docs/troubleshooting-1](https://support.whatfix.com/docs/troubleshooting-1)   
2. Content Inspector(Beta)  
3. ![][image1]  
4. Reach out to Whatfix Support/Solutions/Sales Engineers via relevant channels for more complex issues

### **WFX Customer-facing teams**

Available troubleshooting avenues for internal teams are:

1. Whatfix callbacks/APIs. Some of the most commonly used ones:  
   1. \_wfx\_settings.debug\_mode=true;\_wfx\_refresh()

   2. ![][image2]  
   3. \_wfx\_debug\_finder() \- Primarily for troubleshooting dynamic content

   4. ![][image3]  
   5. \_wfx\_debug\_panel() \- Bare bones visual debugging console

   6. ![][image4]  
2. Internal-only debugging/troubleshooting Confluence documents  
   1. Example: [TroubleShooting Guide](https://whatfix.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/SC/pages/1701445888)  
3. Screen share sessions with customers wherever applicable

## Gaps in existing avenues

|  | For Content Creators | For WFX Customer-facing teams |
| :---: | ----- | ----- |
| 1 | Most content creators are not tech-savvy, issues with content are difficult to understand and troubleshooting documents address only basic scenarios | Debugging methods are technical, browser console based and require oversight |
| 2 | Content Inspector primarily gives information during play of **dynamic content,** doesn’t address scenarios where element captured is within an iFrame and doesn’t give segment info ![][image5] | Broken troubleshooting experience in situations where app access is unavailable. High need for screen time from content creators/end-users to ascertain issues and provide fixes |
| 3 | Content Inspector was also launched as a beta feature and it’s adoption is minimal. [Data](https://whatfix1.cloud.looker.com/explore/amplitude/amplitude?qid=zI9i1MiWvsdalXLi2de7LW&origin_space=183&toggle=fil) | Fragmented debugging/troubleshooting information and techniques. High context switching |
| 4 | Lack of self-serviceability and high dependency on Whatfix Support/Solutions teams increases TAT for an issue | Adoption of debug panel is low, given it’s not very user friendly |

## Competitor Analysis

### Pendo

* Pendo offers a visual debugging tool called the “Pendo Debugger”  
* ![][image6]  
* Creators/end-users can invoke the Debugger only via the browser console; command: pendo.enableDebugging()  
* The Debugger gives information about most of Pendo’s features and functionalities, with the ability to drill down into specifics  
* Reference: [Pendo Web SDK debugger](https://support.pendo.io/hc/en-us/articles/360034229512-Why-is-my-Guide-not-displaying)

### Appcues

* Appcues also offers a visual debugging tool called the “Appcues Debugger”  
* ![][image7]  
* Appcues also offers a “[Diagnostics Tool](https://studio.appcues.com/diagnostics)”, an in-built portal that helps content creators understand flow and checklist(tasklist) eligibility(visibility)  
* ![][image8]  
* The Diagnostics Tool can be accessed via the main Dashboard as well as the Appcues Debugger  
* Appcues Debugger can be invoked via the Appcues Editor extension as well as from the browser console(Appcues.debug())  
* The debugger gives information about why a particular flow isn’t showing or why a particular step isn’t showing up either  
* Reference: [Test and debug your Appcues installation](https://docs.appcues.com/article/34-debugging-your-appcues-installation)

### Walkme

* Walkme has a full-fledged visual debugging assistant called “Flow Tracker“  
* Details are here: [Flow Tracker \- WalkMe Help Center](https://support.walkme.com/knowledge-base/flow-tracker/)  
* ![][image9]  
* Flow Tracker can be used to test and evaluate WalkMe items  
* It is integrated within the Walkme Editor and offers various features  
* There are multiple limitations called out, the primary one being lack of support for Flow Tracker within iFrames  
* Other limitations \- [https://support.walkme.com/knowledge-base/flow-tracker/\#limitations-and-supports](https://support.walkme.com/knowledge-base/flow-tracker/#limitations-and-supports)

### Userlane

* Userlane does not have a visual debugging tool  
* Instead, it focuses on notifying content creators about step/flow failures via “Alerts”  
* ![][image10]  
* This information is exposed in the Analytics dashboard and directly in the Editor(need to verify how)  
* Documentation is to be followed to fix/isolate the issue whenever there’s an alert triggered  
* Reference:[Alert Notification | Userlane Help Center](https://docs.userlane.com/docs/alert-notification)

 

 

## Next Steps

Conduct UT with Design for validation
